Apple is set to introduce a new electrically-induced battery removal technology in the upcoming iPhone 17 series. The innovation promises easier battery detachment with minimal effort.

New battery technology in the iPhone 17 seriesâ€‹According to a recent post by Majin Bu, the entire iPhone 17 lineup, including the iPhone 17, iPhone 17 Air, iPhone 17 Pro, and iPhone 17 Pro Max, will feature a new adhesive technology. This adhesive allows the battery to detach smoothly by applying a small electrical voltage. The change builds on the existing technology seen in the iPhone 16 and iPhone 16 Plus, which also use a similar adhesive mechanism.



This battery removal process lets users detach the battery from the frame with ease. A low-voltage electrical current applied to the adhesive helps the battery come loose quickly. This innovation makes battery maintenance and replacement simpler for users.



Apple first added this technology to the iPhone 16 and iPhone 16 Plus models. Itâ€™s a significant upgrade over traditional adhesive methods. The adhesive in these models loosens with a low-voltage current from a 9V battery, USB-C charger, or other power sources.



The iPhone 16 Pro and iPhone 16 Pro Max models donâ€™t have this battery removal feature yet. But, according to Majin Buâ€™s report, Apple plans to add this technology to all four iPhone 17 models next year.

This removal enhances usabilityâ€‹The introduction of this new adhesive peel technology in the iPhone 17 series marks a significant shift in how Apple handles battery maintenance in its devices. Majin Bu shared an image of the adhesive in different sizes, hinting that it might cater to the varying models within the iPhone 17 lineup.



While other sources have not yet confirmed this news, the expansion of the iPhone 17 battery removal feature seems like a logical progression in Appleâ€™s product development. The shift could lead to easier repairs and replacements, enhancing the user experience.



Majin Bu, who shared the information about the iPhone 17 battery removal feature, has a mixed track record when it comes to Apple leaks. However, not all his predictions have been accurate, such as the rumored iPad 11 release that never materialized.

Later this year, Apple will launch its new iPhone 17 models. The rumors claim that Apple will introduce one new model to its portfolio. This comes in the form of the iPhone 17 Air, a newer and slimmer model. A report from South Korea has now revealed information about the iPhone 17 Air, including its thickness and price.



Going super thinâ€‹In a report from Sisa Journal, we get some details on how thick the iPhone 17 Air is. Apparently, Apple is targeting a thickness of 6.25mm. For context, this is about 20% thinner than the iPhone 16 which measures 7.8mm thin. This means that Apple will have reduced the thickness of the iPhone 17 Air by close to 1.6mm. That is honestly quite impressive. If the rumors are true, then the iPhone 17 Air will be almost 10% thinner than the thinnest iPhone to date, which is the iPhone 6.



Appleâ€™s chief rival, Samsung, doesnâ€™t even come close. The base Galaxy S24 is 7.8mm thick, while the Galaxy S24 Ultra measures 8.6mm thick, thicker than the iPhone 16 Pro.



The report also reveals the potential pricing of the iPhone 17 Air. The report claims the iPhone 17 Air starts at around $800. Prices will likely increase based on storage configuration, but it remains cheaper than the current iPhone 16 Plus, which starts at $900.



Replacing the iPhone 17 Plusâ€‹Rumors suggest the iPhone 17 Air will replace the Plus model while being cheaper than the iPhone 16 Plus. This means that there will not be an iPhone 17 Plus, with the Air model taking its place.



So why would Apple release a cheaper iPhone to replace its more expensive model? This is because Apple could potentially compromise on certain features to achieve the thin form factor. A report from The Information back in 2024 suggested that the iPhone 17 Air will only have a single earpiece speaker at the bottom.



This is versus current iPhone models, some of which have two speakers at the bottom. Rumors suggest Apple will equip the iPhone 17 Air with a single rear camera. The report claims the camera will be centered, an unusual design choice compared to previous iPhone models. Another interesting feature is that the iPhone 17 Air could be the first to feature the use of Appleâ€™s own in-house modems.



In any case, take this report with a grain of salt for now. Only time will tell if the iPhone 17 Airâ€™s thickness and price match the rumors.

The dream of the Trump tariffs is to bring high-tech manufacturing to the U.S. The reality could be a bajillion-dollar iPhone.



Appleâ€™s big moneymaker is a global patchwork. It has components sourced from all over the world, brought together primarily in China, where electronics manufacturing has been perfected over a generation.



Moving just the assembly process to the U.S.? Not cheap and definitely not easy.

Take a look at this iPhone 16 Pro. Your cost, for the 256GB version, is $1,100. The cost of all the hardware insideâ€”aka the bill of materialsâ€”was about $550 to Apple when the phone was introduced, says Wayne Lam, research analyst at TechInsights, which breaks down major products. Throw in assembly and testing and Appleâ€™s cost rises to around $580. Even when you account for Appleâ€™s advertising budget and all the included servicesâ€”iMessage, iCloud, etc.â€”thereâ€™s still a healthy profit margin.



Now factor in the newly announced tariff for goods from China, which currently totals 54%. The cost rises to around $850. That profit margin would shrink dramatically if Apple didnâ€™t up the price. And you donâ€™t become a trillion-dollar gadget company by charging for things at cost.



An Apple spokeswoman declined to comment on the companyâ€™s pricing plans or manufacturing details.

So what about that American-made iPhone? Wouldnâ€™t it at least save on tariffs? Apple would still pay levies on the deviceâ€™s many imported parts. Plus, a manufacturing move to the U.S. would be â€œa massive, mammoth undertakingâ€ that would take years, says Barton Crockett, senior research analyst at brokerage firm Rosenblatt Securities.



And the phone itself would likely cost moreâ€”a lot more. The assembly ecosystem in China is labor intensive and wouldnâ€™t make economic sense in the U.S., Crockett explains. â€œItâ€™s not clear you can make a competitively priced smartphone here.â€



By Lamâ€™s estimates, the assembly labor that might cost $30 per phone in China could cost $300 in the U.S. And if every single component, from the touchscreen display to the internal storage were built here? Yep, a bajillion dollars. And maybe a magic wand.



This tariff matter is far from settled. Our advice is to hold off on stockpiling last yearâ€™s iPhones, and make that one in your hand last as long as it can.

[Twitter Post] https://twitter.com/i/web/status/1918688118968054191"]



In internal testing, Apple determined that the percentage of users who will be able to use the iPhone 17 Air for a full day without needing to recharge the device throughout the day will be between 60% and 70%, according to the report. For other iPhone models, the report said that metric is apparently between 80% and 90%.



The reduced battery life is due in part to the iPhone 17 Air's ultra-thin 5.5mm design, which would make it the thinnest iPhone ever.



To mitigate this problem, the report said that Apple is planning to release a battery case as an optional accessory for the iPhone 17 Air.



Apple last released battery cases for the iPhone 11 lineup, followed by the since-discontinued MagSafe Battery Pack for iPhone 12 models and newer. A battery case both protects an iPhone and provides additional battery life as necessary.

post_content:
If purely for iPhone/Mac, I tend to think iCloud is the way to go: S$3.98 per month for 200GB, S$13.98 per month for 2TB.



If need to have iPhone/Android/Windows/Mac compatibility, Google One may be the way to go. (S$40 per year for 200GB, S$140 per year for 2TB)



But if also need Office 365, then Microsoft 365 family may be a good option (S$148 per year, up to 6 x 1TB Onedrive storage). Office 365 personal is also good if you only need to have one user (up to 1TB Onedrive storage). Still I feel Onedrive is not the best app for photo backup.
